1:
The chapter opens with Saul's restless malice, and the grammatical-historical commentators set the stage. Ellicott marks how intent the king was on his bloody purpose: no sooner was the Philistine raid repulsed than, with sleepless animosity, he set out to hunt his supposed rival, encouraged by the Ziphites who had spied out David's lurking-places (1 Sam. 23:19). He fills the margin with En-gedi itself — still called Ain-jedy, the Fountain of the Kid — a beautiful oasis whose older name was Hazazon Tamar (Gen. 14:7), the garden Solomon loved and planted with vines, likening his beloved to a cluster of camphire in its vineyards (Song 1:14). Keil explains that the rocks of the wild goats is probably no proper name but a general term for a region so thick with mountain goats and chamois that they still haunt it.

The wesleyan voices add their own touches to the picture. Clarke supposes the Philistine incursion was only a small marauding party, soon suppressed, which freed Saul to turn again upon David. Wesley dwells on the terrain: these rocks are exceeding steep, full of precipices, and dangerous even to travellers, as an eye-witness had left on record; yet Saul was so transported with rage that he ventured himself and his army among them to take a man who, he thought, would count himself safe in such inaccessible places.

The puritan Matthew Henry presses the moral wonder of it. No sooner had Saul come home safe from chasing the Philistines than he inquired after David to do him a mischief, as if he had been delivered on purpose to do all these abominations (Jer. 7:10). The frequent incursions of the enemy might have shown him how needful it was to recall David and restore him to the army; but so far from this, he is now more than ever exasperated, and draws out three thousand choice men to pursue one poor exile upon the rocks of the wild goats, where, one would think, David need be envied neither a habitation nor a moment's peace.

The reformed Gill supplies the plain sense that Saul had returned from following the Philistines, having driven them out of his country. The witnesses do not divide; together they open the scene as a study in unwearied hatred hunting an innocent man into the desert — while, unseen, Providence prepares the very cave in which the hunter will fall into his victim's power.
2:
The notice of Saul's three thousand chosen men becomes for the witnesses a measure of the king's fear and a small exercise in place-names. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the force. Ellicott reads the large, carefully selected force as an indication how thoroughly Saul was impressed with David's power at this juncture: he plainly looked on him as a rival king who must be met by a numerous and disciplined army. Keil, following Van de Velde, would locate the sheep-folds by the road in the desolate Wady Chareitun, a defile of rocky, precipitous walls rising many hundred feet, habitable by little but wild goats.

A quiet translation question runs beneath the verse: is the rocks of the wild goats a description or a proper name? Clarke lays out the variety. The Vulgate paraphrases 'the most precipitous rocks over which the ibexes alone can travel'; the Targum reads 'the caverns of the rocks'; while the Septuagint turns the Hebrew into a place-name, producing forms such as Saddaiem, which he judges evidently corruptions of the original. Ellicott notes the same possibility — that some have taken it as a proper name — but keeps to the descriptive sense, 'ibex rocks,' so called because only these chamois of Syria would find pasturage on them, and the ibex is still found among those cliffs near Ain-jedy.

The wesleyan Wesley gives the verse its human colour, picturing rocks that the wild goats delight to climb, and a Saul so mastered by rage that he would risk an army on their dangerous precipices to catch his prey. The reformed Gill simply grounds the muster, noting that the three thousand were taken out of the army with which Saul had been pursuing the Philistines. There is no real dispute here; the witnesses agree that the disproportion is itself eloquent — a whole corps of picked troops sent by a reigning king to run down a single fugitive among the goat-tracks, and they let the very numbers testify against the persecutor before David ever speaks. Ellicott presses the point home: Saul so evidently reckoned David a king in the making that he would meet him only with a force 'numerous and disciplined,' as though marching against a rival throne rather than an exiled harper. That the whole apparatus of state was turned against one landless man is, for the witnesses, the first silent indictment of the hunt, and it prepares the reader to feel the wonder of what David will refuse to do when the mighty king falls, defenceless, into his hand.
3:
Here the witnesses divide over a single delicate phrase — Saul went in to cover his feet — while agreeing on the darkness that made David's mercy possible. The larger point of debate is what Saul entered the cave to do. The wesleyan Clarke, with the Vulgate, the Septuagint, the Targum, and the Arabic, takes the phrase in its plainer physical sense, of relieving nature, adding that afterward he lay down to rest and so slept while David crept near. Against this the grammatical-historical Ellicott argues, with the Peshito Syriac, Michaelis, and Ewald, that the words mean simply that Saul, fatigued with the morning's march, withdrew about midday to take a short siesta in one of those cool, silent caves; he reasons that the ordinary interpretation does not suit the narrative, since considerable time was needed for the sentinel to warn David and for David to steal up, cut the mantle, and retire. Wesley stands with the sleep reading, noting that Saul, a military man used to sleeping on the ground and weary with his eager pursuit, both needed and desired rest, God also disposing him to it that David might have this eminent occasion to prove his integrity.

On the setting itself the commentators are one. Gill and Clarke describe the sheepcotes as caves in the rock where shepherds and their flocks still lodge, and marshal the ancient witnesses to their vast size — Strabo's cavern in Syria said to hold four thousand men, and Pococke's grotto called El Maamah, the hiding-place, into which tradition says thirty thousand once retired from a bad air. Ellicott adds, from Thomson, the crucial optical fact: coming in from the glare of day one cannot see four paces forward, but those already within, looking outward, see with perfect distinctness all that passes at the mouth. So David could watch Saul while Saul saw only impenetrable darkness.

Clarke cannot resist the rabbinic embellishment that God caused a spider to weave her web across the cave's mouth, so that Saul, seeing it, entered without suspicion — a Jewish tradition he calls one of the most elegant in their collection, and a reminder that even a spider in God's hand may work a great salvation. The debate over covering the feet is real but bounded; whether Saul stooped to nature or lay down to sleep, all agree he was wholly at David's mercy in the dark, and that the providence which led him there is the true subject of the verse.
4:
David's men urge him to strike, and the witnesses probe both their theology and David's own heart. The grammatical-historical commentators expose the flaw in the men's reasoning. Keil insists that no word of Jehovah had in fact come to David: the speakers had merely taken the leadings of providence, by which Saul was brought into David's power, as though they were a divine intimation, and boldly dressed their own inference in the language of a promise, 'Behold the day of which the Lord said unto thee.' Ellicott traces the supposed oracle to real predictions David's men knew — that the kingdom would be his (1 Sam. 15:28; 16:12) — words echoed in Jonathan's assurances (1 Sam. 23:17), though he allows that a prophet such as Gad may even have foretold David's reign publicly in the hearing of the band.

A sharper question is what David himself intended when he 'arose.' The wesleyan Clarke reads the circumstances plainly: David rose at his men's word to take Saul's life, and it was in reference to this deadly purpose that his heart would soon smite him; only when about to do it was he prevented by the remonstrance of God in his conscience, so that instead of the head, which the laws of war would have allowed, he took only the skirt. Ellicott dramatizes the same moment — for an instant David listened to the seductive voice of the tempter, the sword of Goliath in his hand, advancing toward the sleeper to end the long war with one blow — but only for an instant, for the thought crossed his soul that the helpless man was the Anointed of the Lord, and with a matchless generosity he stooped instead to cut a corner of the royal robe.

The wesleyan and reformed voices agree with Keil against any divine warrant. Wesley says flatly that God had made no such promise; the men put their own construction upon the promises of deliverance, treating them as an obligation to seize every opportunity that offered. Gill names the men as David's principal officers, some reckoning Joab and Abishai among them. The witnesses do not finally divide: they hold together that Providence had indeed given the opportunity, but that opportunity is not command, and that the men mistook a permission of God for a precept — the very error David's conscience will correct in the verses that follow.
5:
This short verse turns on a tender crux — why did David's heart smite him? — and the witnesses give two answers. The reformed and grammatical-historical commentators read the pang as remorse for the affront to the king. Gill says simply that his conscience accused him and he repented of what he had done. Keil agrees: his heart smote him after the deed because he regarded even the cutting of the robe as an injury done to the king himself, and the greater firmness with which he then restrained his men grew out of that very tenderness of conscience. Matthew Henry, from his survey of the scene, strikes the same note — though the act did Saul no real hurt and served David as proof that his life had been in his hand, yet because it was an affront to the royal dignity he wished he had not done it; and Henry draws the lesson that it is a good thing to have a heart smiting us for sins that seem little, a sign of a conscience awake and tender.

Ellicott presses a deeper reading. David's heart, he urges, smote him not for what he had done to Saul's garment but for the momentary thought that had stained his soul — the thought of slaying the Lord's Anointed. He rejects the milder view of Clericus, that David merely feared Saul would take the cutting as a violation of majesty, and finds no sign at all that David regretted the fringe; it was the far more terrible impulse toward murder, checked in an instant, that troubled him, as the solemn words of the next verse confirm.

The Talmud, which Ellicott records, condemns the act more severely still: Rabbi Yosi ben Chanina taught that whoever treats clothes slightingly will at last derive no benefit from them, citing how David in his old age was covered with clothes yet gat no heat (1 Kgs 1:1) — a cryptic way of teaching reverence for the guides of Israel, here represented by Saul. Whether the reproach fell on the robe or on the buried wish to kill, the witnesses agree on the thing itself: a conscience so fine that it trembled at the least breach of reverence toward the man God had once anointed, and that tenderness is the seed of all David's restraint.
6:
David's oath — the Lord forbid that I should do this thing unto my master, the Lord's anointed — draws from the witnesses a single, unanimous doctrine: the sacredness of a divinely appointed office. The grammatical-historical Keil hears in the vehemence of the refusal a decisive proof against his men's theology: with all the greater firmness David repelled their suggestion, and these very words show clearly that no word of Jehovah had come to him giving him leave to deal with Saul as he pleased. The oath is not calculation but abhorrence — a holy 'far be it from me on the Lord's account.'

The wesleyan Clarke draws out the principle at length. However unworthily Saul was now acting, he had been appointed to his high office by God himself and could be removed only by the authority that placed him on the throne. Even David, who knew he was appointed to reign in Saul's stead and whose life Saul had often sought, conceived that he had no right to take that life; and he grounds his forbearance on two facts — Saul is his master and he a subject, and Saul is the Lord's anointed, sacred in his person before the Lord. It is an awful thing, Clarke insists, to kill a king once constitutionally set on the throne; no such experiment ever succeeds, the Lord abhors king-killing, and had David struck the blow he would in the sight of God have been a murderer.

The reformed Gill sets the saying in its place in the action, whether spoken as David returned with the skirt in his hand or in answer to the men when they first pressed him to dispatch his enemy. The witnesses raise no debate. They stand together on the ground David takes: he weighs Saul now not as the one man who blocks his advancement — which would only tempt him — but as the Lord's anointed and as his own master, under the special protection of the divine law, and lets that double consideration bind his hand. What might have been the day of David's crowning becomes instead the day of his self-command, and the verse stands as the chapter's moral centre.
7:
The witnesses read this verse as David's restraint reaching beyond himself to govern his men. The grammatical-historical Keil catches the force of the Hebrew: David 'kept back his people with words,' the verb suggesting that he had almost to tear them apart from their purpose, and so did not suffer them to rise up against Saul to slay him — upon which Saul, all unknowing, rose out of the cave and went on his way. The whole crisis passes in the space of a breath, the king's life hanging on one man's word to a handful of eager soldiers.

The wesleyan Clarke fastens on David's responsibility for those under his command. Because he could restrain them it was his duty to do so; had he merely stood by and connived at their killing Saul, David himself would have been the murderer. Clarke turns from the cave to the throne-room, recalling that in prayer the Church calls God the only Ruler of princes, since their authority is the highest among men and God alone stands above them, and he quotes the old line of Horace that kings are supreme over their own subjects while Jove is supreme over kings themselves. The point is that David, holding a lawful authority of his own, will not let it be used to overthrow the higher one.

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the moment to its highest key. David would not only refuse the evil deed himself but would not suffer those about him to do it; and in this, Henry says, he rendered good for evil to the man from whom he had received evil for good, showing himself both a type of Christ, who saved his persecutors, and an example to all Christians not to be overcome of evil but to overcome evil with good (Rom. 12:21). The reformed Gill adds only that David pacified his men and made them quiet, reconciling them to his conduct by the plea that Saul was the anointed of the Lord. There is no division among the witnesses — they agree that true forbearance is not passive but active, mastering not one's own hand only but the hands of others.
8:
David steps from the cave to plead, and the witnesses dwell on the depth of reverence with which he does it. The grammatical-historical commentators frame the scene. Ellicott pictures the outlaw suffering the king and his companion to proceed a little way down the ascent before he calls after him, but with an address of the deepest reverence, and accompanied by an act of the profoundest homage an inferior could pay a superior — he would show Saul that, whatever the court believed, he was no rival king. Keil marks the purpose behind the courtesy: David called 'My lord king' that, when Saul looked round, he might expostulate with him in the deepest reverence yet with earnest words, sharpening the king's conscience as to the groundlessness of his suspicion and the injustice of the pursuit.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the appeal as the answer of innocence to slander. David was falsely charged with seeking Saul's hurt; now he shows Saul that God's providence had actually put the king's life in his hand, and yet upon a good principle he had refused to take it. Henry underlines David's fixed resolution never to be his own avenger, content to leave his cause with God, who will right the wronged at farthest in the judgment of the great day. The bow to the earth is thus no empty ceremony but the outward sign of a heart that will not lift itself against the throne.

The reformed Gill simply notes that David arose and went out only after Saul was gone, so that his appeal came from a safe distance yet in full view, the skirt of the robe ready in his hand as proof. The witnesses agree without contention: this is a man wronged addressing the one who wronged him, and doing it with a homage so genuine that the very posture of his body preaches what his words will urge — that he honours the king even while the king hunts his life, and that his quarrel, if quarrel there be, he commits to a higher Judge. The stooping face and the outstretched hand together disarm the charge before a syllable of self-defence is spoken.
9:
David's opening question — wherefore hearest thou men's words? — becomes for the witnesses a lesson in how a good man deals gently with a king he must yet reprove. The reformed Gill observes that David chose to lay the blame on Saul's courtiers rather than on Saul himself: the false charges and accusations had been carried by sycophants and flatterers such as Doeg the Edomite, and by fixing the fault on them David sought to reconcile the king and win a hearing for what he had to say. It is the tact of a man who will tell the truth without needlessly provoking the one who holds his life.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott supplies the court intrigue behind the words. David had many deadly enemies about Saul who laboured with success to deepen the king's jealousy and widen the breach — Doeg among the more prominent, and Cush the Benjamite, the slanderer named in the inscription that heads the seventh psalm (Ps. 7), together with the Ziphites and their spokesmen at the royal residence. Keil reads the appeal as David's opening thrust, calmly asking why the king should listen to men who say, 'Behold, David seeketh thy hurt,' when the events of that very day gave the lie to the charge.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the courtesy and its ground. It is a piece of respect due to crowned heads, he says, that when they do amiss we charge it upon those about them who advise them ill; David had reason enough to think Saul persecuted him from his own envy, yet he generously supposes that others had set him on and made him believe David an enemy. Behind such whisperers Henry sees Satan, the great accuser of the brethren, who keeps his agents in the courts of princes that give them ear, dressing up the people of God in bear-skins that they may be baited. Henry adds that David had every reason to think Saul persecuted him from pure envy and malice, yet courteously chose to lay the fault elsewhere, teaching a hard charity that will excuse where it honestly can. The witnesses do not divide; together they show David sparing the king's person even in his speech, aiming his reproof past the throne at the flatterers who poison it, so that the very form of his complaint leaves Saul a way back to himself.
10:
David turns from the flatterers to the fact, and the witnesses concentrate on the evidence of the morning and on a single disputed clause. The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the scene with precision: David and a crowd of armed men stood at the mouth of the cave the king had just left, so that Saul's own eye had seen — was that very moment seeing — that his life had lately been in his enemy's hand. The proof needs no argument; it lies open before the king's face.

The clause and some bade me kill thee carries a real textual crux, and Ellicott lays out the options. The literal Hebrew runs that Jehovah delivered Saul into David's hand and 'bade kill' him, a reading some have defended by treating God's leading of Saul into the cave as itself a direction to strike; but this, he judges, runs against the whole spirit of the narrative. The English Version, following the Syriac and Chaldee, supplies 'some' — one among David's men — as the speaker who bade the killing, which gives the sense the Hebrews have always found here; while the Vulgate, with a slight change of the vowel-points, renders 'I thought to kill thee.' In the next clause, mine eye spared thee, the subject is again supplied, Ellicott preferring 'my soul' or 'my hand' to the received 'mine eye.'

The reformed Gill keeps to the plain thrust, that this day Saul's own eyes had full proof of David's forbearance, presently to be shown in the skirt. Beneath the grammar the witnesses are agreed on the substance: David does not merely claim mercy, he demonstrates it, resting his whole defence on a thing Saul cannot deny — that God had given the king into his power, that a voice at his elbow had urged the fatal stroke, and that he had held his hand for one reason only, because he would not put it forth against the Lord's anointed. The disputed word changes nothing of the deed; whoever spoke, David refused. Ellicott stresses how complete the proof was: the king was not asked to take David's word for his loyalty but to read it in the plain fact of his own survival, standing alive before the very men who could have killed him. The grammar of the clause may be argued, but the argument does not touch the substance, for on any reading the sense is the same — that God had put the king wholly in his rival's power, that the killing was urged, and that David alone stayed the blow out of reverence for the anointing that still lay upon his enemy's head.
11:
The held-up skirt and the word my father give the witnesses both a moving image and a small debate. The debate concerns the address. The reformed Gill takes my father in a double sense: Saul was David's father naturally, as having given him his daughter to wife, and civilly, as a king who ought to treat his subjects, David among them, as his children. The grammatical-historical Ellicott demurs from the first sense: the Princess Michal had most likely already been given to Phalti (1 Sam. 25:44) before the cave, the relations of David and Saul being far more bitter before this meeting than after; so 'my father,' he holds, is simply the reverence of the young toward the old, of the loyal subject toward his sovereign.

On the gesture itself the witnesses are one. Ellicott pictures David at this juncture holding up the piece of the royal mantle he had so carefully cut, as if to say, 'See how near thou wast to death, had I been pleased to take thy life when I cut this off.' Keil hears in the same word my father the expression of a childlike reverence and affection David cherished toward the anointed of the Lord, and reads the whole plea as an appeal to Saul to learn and see, from the skirt and the spared life, that there was neither evil nor transgression in David's hand.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the protest into its parts. David calls him father, from whom one may look for compassion; he solemnly avows his own innocence — no evil nor transgression in his hand, whatever he may have sinned against God — and produces an unexceptionable witness to it, the skirt itself: had the charge against him been true, he says in effect, Saul's head would now be in his hand and not merely the hem of his robe, for he could as easily have taken the one as the other. The witnesses agree that the token in David's hand is worth more than any oath; it turns the accusation inside out, making the very proof of David's power the proof of his loyalty.
12:
With the Lord judge between me and thee David commits his cause to heaven, and the witnesses read the words as at once an appeal and a renunciation. The reformed Gill hears David refusing to be judge in his own quarrel: he asks God to make it appear who is in the right and who in the wrong, and so leaves the whole matter to be determined by divine providence rather than by his own avenging hand. It is the language of a man who believes his innocence needs no defender but God.

The grammatical-historical Keil draws out the logic of the saying. Having proved that Saul had no ground whatever for seeking his life, David could very calmly invoke the Lord as judge to decide the cause and deliver him out of the king's hand; and the second half of the verse guards the first. 'Jehovah will avenge me upon thee, but my hand shall not be against thee,' David says, and appeals to the old proverb, 'Evil proceedeth from the evil.' Keil catches its bearing exactly: only a wicked man could wish to take his own revenge, and by refusing it David shows he is not that man. The renunciation of vengeance is thus also a self-vindication.

The puritan Matthew Henry twice marks this as David's fixed resolution never to be his own avenger — 'the Lord avenge me of thee, but mine hand shall not be upon thee' — and finds in it the settled comfort of oppressed innocence, that the justice of God is its refuge; if men wrong us, God will right us, at the furthest in the judgment of the great day. With that, Henry says, David leaves his cause and rests satisfied, waiting God's time to appear for him. The witnesses do not differ; they agree that David here does two things at once, casting himself wholly upon God's judgment and, by that very act, disproving the slander that he sought the king's hurt. He will have a kingdom from God's hand or not at all, but never one seized by his own.
13:
The proverb of the ancients — 'Wickedness proceedeth from the wicked' — gives the witnesses a maxim to unfold and a curious question about its source. The reformed Gill lingers on the phrase itself. It may mean the proverb of the ancient one, that is of the oldest man, the first man Adam, from whom such wisdom descended; or, as some read it in the Talmud, of the Ancient One of the world, the Ancient of days, the Lord himself. Whichever, the saying is old and fitly applied to the present case.

The grammatical-historical and wesleyan voices agree on the point of the maxim: deeds reveal the doer. Keil reads it as David's argument that only a wicked man would wish to avenge himself, adding that even had David wished to attack the king he had not the power. Ellicott cites Clericus and Grotius, that a man's actions usually answer to the quality of his mind, so that from a heart guilty of conspiracy nothing but corresponding deeds could come forth, and he sets beside it the Greek proverb Erdmann quotes, 'From a bad raven comes a bad egg.' Clarke turns the saying on David's own conduct: he who does a wicked act proves himself wicked, and had David been the conspirator Saul's courtiers described, he would not have neglected so fair an opportunity to kill.

Wesley states it most plainly of all: wicked men do wicked actions, among which the killing of one's own sovereign lord and king is one; and therefore, had David been so wicked a person as the courtiers represented, he would have made no conscience of laying violent hands on Saul. The witnesses do not quarrel over the proverb's origin; the small uncertainty whether the 'ancient' is Adam, the Ancient of days, or simply antiquity leaves the lesson untouched. All draw the same conclusion — that David's spared hand is itself the surest disproof of the charge against him, since a wicked heart, given the chance, betrays itself in a wicked deed, and David's chance had come and gone without a wound. The maxim, Keil observes, cuts two ways at once: it clears David, who did not avenge himself, and it quietly warns Saul, whose own restless pursuit is the very wickedness that proceeds from a wicked purpose. David turns an old saw into an argument, letting the accumulated wisdom of the ancients testify that the man who spared his enemy in the dark can be no plotter against his life.
14:
David's homely similes — a dead dog, a flea — draw from the witnesses one voice on his self-abasement and the disproportion of the king's pursuit. The wesleyan Clarke reads the figures as expressions of the most sovereign contempt in Hebrew speech: to call oneself a dead dog was to own oneself utterly beneath notice (compare 2 Sam. 16:9), one incapable of the least resistance against Saul and the armed might of Israel, and 'a flea' says the same again. He notes that the Targum rightly renders both, 'one who is weak, one who is contemptible.'

The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the picture. Keil explains that a dead dog can neither bite nor hurt and is a thing beneath a king's notice (compare 2 Sam. 9:8), while the point of the flea is sheer insignificance (1 Sam. 26:20); by both David paints himself as a harmless and negligible man of whom Saul had no cause to be afraid and whom the king of Israel should think it beneath his dignity to chase. Ellicott adds that such vivid similes are common in Oriental discourse, and that the original is stronger still — not merely a flea but 'one flea,' a single flea, which is not easily caught, easily escapes, and if caught is poor game for a royal hunter.

The puritan Matthew Henry presses the shame of it upon Saul. It was below so great a king to enter the lists with one so unequal a match — one of his own servants, bred a poor shepherd, now an exile neither able nor willing to resist — for to conquer such a foe would be no honour and to attempt it a disparagement. David was so far from aspiring, Henry observes, that in his own account he was but a dead dog; and he recalls the wisdom of the ancients, that the eagle does not dart upon flies and that it is enough for the lion to have laid his victim low. The witnesses do not divide: David's lowliness is real, and it is also an argument, meant to touch whatever spark of generosity remained in the king by showing how little glory his hunt could earn.
15:
David closes his appeal by handing the whole cause to God, and the witnesses read the verse as the rest of a quiet conscience. The reformed Gill hears David disowning any wish to be judge in his own quarrel, content to leave the determining of it to God, who would settle it for him in his providence. The repeated cry — 'the Lord be judge, and judge between me and thee, and see, and plead my cause' — is not impatience but committal, the appeal of a man who has said all he means to say and now falls silent before heaven.

The grammatical-historical Keil gathers the argument to its conclusion. Since Saul had no good ground for the persecution, David could very calmly commit his cause to the Lord God, that He might decide it as judge and deliver him out of the king's hand: 'Let Him look at it, and conduct my cause.' The verb, Keil notes, asks God both to see the wrong and to take up the pleading of it, so that David seeks not merely acquittal but an advocate. Clarke states the same in a breath — let God determine who is guilty — and so lays the matter down.

Ellicott crowns the verse with the words of Bishop Sanderson, which put David's whole mind into a sentence: 'Shall I lay these hands on the Lord's Anointed? God forbid. No; I will not do it for a kingdom. Such wicked feats I leave for wicked men to act. God can and will in His own due time make good His own promise without my sin. I shall be content to wait His leisure, and remain in the sad condition I now am in, till it shall please Him to bring me out of it.' The witnesses do not contend; they agree that David's refusal of vengeance and his appeal to the Judge are one act, and that the justice of God is the true refuge of oppressed innocence — a man may keep a good conscience and safely leave his vindication with the God who searches the heart.
16:
Saul's broken cry — is this thy voice, my son David? — and his weeping become for the witnesses a study in feeling that is real yet not repentance. The reformed Gill traces the wonder that Saul, so full of fury, should stand and hear David out; he ascribes it to the restraining providence of God, to the surprise of seeing David emerge, and above all to the skirt in David's hand, a sure token that his life had been spared, which made the king listen. The wesleyan Wesley notes that Saul knew the voice though at that distance he could not discern the face, and that he wept from the sense of his sin against God and his base carriage toward David.

The puritan Matthew Henry weighs the tears with care. He will not suppose them counterfeit but takes them as real expressions of Saul's present concern at the sight of his own iniquity so plainly proved; the king speaks as one quite overcome with David's kindness and relenting at his own folly and ingratitude. Yet Henry adds the sober qualification that many mourn for their sins who do not truly repent of them, weeping bitterly and yet continuing in love and league with them. So Saul's melting was genuine as far as it went, and went no further than the moment.

The grammatical-historical Keil observes that David's conduct went to Saul's heart, so that he wept aloud and confessed, 'Thou art more righteous than I.' Ellicott, quoting Philippson, catches the grandeur of the scene — David standing on a peak of the wilderness, holding up the trophy of his generosity and addressing the melancholy king he loved as a father, paid homage to as a king, and reverenced as the Lord's Anointed, while Saul, for his part, had hunted him without a cause. There is nothing strange, Ellicott adds, in so sudden a change of feeling in one so nervous and excitable; but the sad sequel shows the impression only transitory, and David saw as much, for he made no effort to return home. The witnesses do not divide: they honour the reality of Saul's emotion and, in the same breath, mark its shallowness, a conviction that touched the eyes but not the will.
17:
Saul's confession — thou art more righteous than I — is measured by the witnesses for exactly how far it reaches, and how far it falls short. The reformed Gill fastens on the comparative. By owning David the more righteous, Saul betrays that he still thought himself righteous, only less so; the enemy himself being judge acknowledges David's glaring righteousness, yet will not confess his own wickedness, having no true sense of sin nor any real sorrow for it. The word wrings from Saul an admission about David without a genuine self-accusation.

The grammatical-historical Keil reads the confession as the measure of how deeply David's conduct had gone to the king's heart, drawing from him the acknowledgment, 'Thou hast rewarded me good, whereas I have rewarded thee evil.' The plain antithesis of good repaid for evil is Saul's own, and it is true as far as it is spoken; the deed of the cave had for the moment overpowered the evil spirit in him and forced the better word to his lips.

The wesleyan Wesley gives the gentlest reading, taking Saul to acknowledge ingenuously both David's integrity and his own iniquity. Here the witnesses lie in a faint tension rather than open debate: Wesley hears a frank owning of guilt, while Gill and Matthew Henry insist the confession stops short of true penitence, since Saul would say only 'thou art more righteous than I' and not 'thou art righteous, but I am wicked.' Bad men, Henry remarks, commonly go no further than to grant that others are better and more righteous than they. The witnesses agree on the heart of it: the confession is honest so far as it goes, a real tribute to David extorted by a real kindness, but it is comparison, not contrition, and a man may praise the righteousness of another while sparing his own conscience the harder verdict. Gill draws the sobering implication that Saul's tears and confession, real as they were, left his heart unhumbled: he could weep at David's goodness without ever bowing under his own guilt, and so the very words that seem to end the quarrel prove how little the quarrel had changed him. The witnesses let the comparative stand as a warning — that it is possible to be moved, even to be truthful about another's worth, and still to withhold the one confession that saves, which is not 'thou art better than I' but 'God be merciful to me a sinner.'
18:
Saul presses his own confession further — thou hast showed this day how that thou hast dealt well with me — and here the witnesses are few but of one mind. The reformed Gill points to the proof that had wrung the words from the king: the cutting off of the skirt only, when David held Saul's very life in his hand, was a clear demonstration of his good dealing and enough to convince the king that David had no ill design upon him. The evidence and the confession answer each other; the spared life speaks, and Saul cannot but own what it says.

The grammatical-historical Keil supplies the ground Saul himself lays down in the same breath — 'forasmuch as, when the Lord had delivered me into thine hand, thou killedst me not.' The king's argument is that the deed was no accident of David's weakness but a mercy shown when the power to destroy was complete; the Lord had given Saul over, and David had let him go. It is the very point David had urged in the cave, now returning from the king's own mouth as the admission of his defeated suspicion.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the human lesson from the reversal. Saul here owns himself to have been under a mistake concerning David, seeing that he was so far from seeking the king's hurt that he had dealt well with him; and Henry turns it upon us all, that we are too apt to suspect others worse affected toward us than they really are, or than they prove to be, so that when our mistake is discovered we should be as forward to recall our suspicions as Saul is here. The witnesses do not differ. Together they let this short verse stand as the fruit of David's forbearance in the cave: a kindness so undeniable that even a jealous and hostile king is forced, against the whole grain of his fear, to confess it aloud, and to name as good the man he had come out to destroy. It is the moment when the slander of years collapses under a single fact, and Saul, who had listened to men saying David sought his hurt, must now say with his own mouth that David dealt well with him. The verse thus closes the loop David opened in the cave: the deed he did in the dark is confessed in the open by the very man it spared.
19:
Saul's question — if a man find his enemy, will he let him go well away? — draws from the witnesses a common wonder at David's magnanimity and one small textual note. The reformed Gill reads the king's meaning plainly: it is not usual among men that one who has his enemy in his power should let him depart in peace and safety, unhurt; yet this was the very thing before Saul's eyes, for David had found his enemy and let him go well away. The rarity of the mercy is itself the proof of David's heart.

The grammatical-historical Keil takes the words as a question demanding the answer 'no' — a man does not commonly let his enemy escape unharmed — so that the unspoken sequel is, 'but thou hast dealt far otherwise with me,' upon which follows Saul's prayer, 'the Lord repay thee good for what thou hast done to me this day.' The wesleyan Wesley reads that petition as the confession of a man who felt himself unable to recompense so great a favour and therefore turned it over to God, praying that heaven would reward what he could not.

Clarke adds the one point of textual interest. Following Houbigant and the ancient versions — the Septuagint, Syriac, and Arabic — he would fill out the sentence to read, 'If a man, finding his enemy, send him by a good way, the Lord will give him his reward,' the words in italic being absent from the received Hebrew yet needed, he thinks, to complete the sense and to introduce Saul's blessing. The witnesses do not divide over the substance. They agree that Saul, however transient his mood, here pronounces David's act beyond the common measure of men and, having no gift equal to it, does the one thing left to him — he commits the recompense of David's kindness to the God whom David had just made his judge, so that the same heaven is asked to reward the mercy that had been asked to try the cause. There is a quiet justice in the symmetry, Gill's plain reading and Keil's rhetorical question converging on it: David had appealed to God as judge and avenger, and now Saul, disarmed, can only echo the appeal in the language of blessing, praying that the Lord will pay what the king himself is too poor, and perhaps too changeable, to render.
20:
Saul's remarkable avowal — I know well that thou shalt surely be king — sets the witnesses to ask how the king came by so sure a knowledge. The grammatical-historical commentators divide the grounds. Keil takes the wish as spoken in perfect sincerity: David's behaviour had for the moment conquered the evil demon of Saul's heart and wholly altered his feelings, so that in this better mood he uttered the conviction he could never quite suppress — that David would reign and the kingdom be established in his hand — the same conviction which, when the evil spirit regained the upper hand, had driven him to the persecution. Clarke renders the Hebrew emphatically, 'reigning thou shalt reign,' and marvels that Saul knew this all along and yet had hunted him.

Ellicott looks behind the magnanimity of the cave to a deeper source. Clericus's remark, that a man so far superior in soul to kings could not but reign, expresses part of Saul's mind; but more lay behind. Saul had never forgotten Samuel's solemn word that the Lord had rent the kingdom from him and given it to a neighbour better than he (1 Sam. 15:28); rumours of a mysterious anointing, the passionate advocacy of Jonathan, and the steady friendship of Samuel had long convinced the king that in the son of Jesse he saw Israel's future monarch.

The reformed Gill joins both strands and adds a striking touch. Saul knew it, he says, by the providence that preserved David, by his princely spirit, and by Samuel's warning that the kingdom would pass to a better neighbour; and he records the Jewish tradition that the very cutting of the skirt recalled the rending of Samuel's mantle by which that word had been sealed, some even reporting that Samuel had said the man who cut off the skirt should reign. The witnesses do not finally divide; whether Saul reasoned from David's noble soul or remembered the prophet's sentence, they agree that the truth was forced from him — that he owned, in a moment of clear sight, the very kingship he had spent his strength to prevent.
21:
Saul's request for an oath — that thou wilt not cut off my seed after me — opens for the witnesses onto a grim Eastern custom and a sharp contrast of characters. The grammatical-historical Ellicott explains the fear. It was a barbarous but common practice in the jealous East for a new dynasty to exterminate the royal house it displaced, and Saul dreaded exactly this for his children; the same dread had moved Jonathan to make David's mercy to his seed a term of their covenant (1 Sam. 20:15). Ellicott heaps up the later instances — Baasha smiting all the house of Jeroboam (1 Kgs 15:29), Zimri all the house of Baasha (1 Kgs 16:11), Jehu all that remained to Ahab (2 Kgs 10) — and finds in Saul's very fear a part of his punishment. Keil adds that a name is exterminated when the whole line is destroyed.

The wesleyan Clarke draws the contrast that gives the verse its edge. Saul knew that an oath would bind David, though it had never been sufficient to bind himself: he had sworn to Jonathan that David should not be slain (1 Sam. 19:6) and yet had sought by every means to destroy him. The king who broke his own oaths must lean on the conscience of the man he persecuted for the safety of his house.

The reformed Gill notes only that David swore by the Lord, while Keil observes that David, even as he gave the oath, remained upon the mountain heights, not trusting the king's passing change of feeling to last. The witnesses do not divide; they let the request expose both men at once — Saul, so sure now of David's coming reign that he begs mercy for his children from the very rival he cannot stop hunting, and David, so known for his conscience that a jealous king will rest the future of his family on nothing surer than the outlaw's word before God. Ellicott finds in this dread no idle fancy but a real anticipation of history, for the annals of the kingdom would soon be stained with just such wholesale slaughters of a fallen house; and he counts it no small part of Saul's own punishment that he must live in fear for the children he loved. The witnesses let the request stand as a last, revealing self-portrait of the king — clutching at David's conscience for the safety he could no longer secure by his crown, and confessing, in the very form of his plea, that the future belonged to the man he had hunted.
22:
The chapter ends with an oath kept and a parting that trusts nothing, and the witnesses read both the faithfulness and the wariness. The reformed Gill dwells on the oath David swore not to cut off Saul's posterity, and how religiously he observed it — sparing Mephibosheth, punishing the murderers of Ishbosheth — while noting that the seven sons given up to the Gibeonites fell not by any breach of the vow but by the express appointment of God, to whom the oaths of men are subject. The puritan Matthew Henry adds that though David might have been tempted, in revenge or even in prudence, to root out Saul's family, he bound himself not to do it, knowing that God could establish the kingdom without such bloody methods.

On the parting the witnesses agree that David was right to keep his distance. Clarke pictures Saul going home confounded and overwhelmed by David's generosity, yet observes that David could not trust him: Saul was no longer under the divine guidance, an evil spirit had full dominion, and 'what God fills not, the devil will occupy.' Wesley says David withdrew to the hold of En-gedi because, taught by frequent experience of Saul's inconstancy, he would trust him no more. Henry warns that it is dangerous to venture on the mercy of a reconciled enemy, and likens David to the Lord, who would not commit himself to men because he knew what was in them (John 2:24); those who like David are innocent as doves must also be wise as serpents (Matt. 10:16).

The one eastern-antiochene voice lifts the scene to allegory. Ellicott records the homily of Chrysostom, preserved by Wordsworth, in which the cave becomes like a Christian church and David like a bishop who first preaches a sermon and then offers the sacrifice of the altar: by his example he preached, and by mastering his own anger he offered a true, spiritual sacrifice — himself — becoming as it were priest, sacrifice, and altar together, and so gaining a glorious victory. The witnesses do not divide; they close the chapter on a man who honoured his word to a faithless king, guarded himself against a mercy he knew to be a mood, and turned even his forbearance in the cave into an offering, the conquest of his own vengeance counted the truest triumph of the day.
